county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1869 from unorganized territory. Named for Maj. Andrew Dawson, fur trader. Glendive was a Northern Pacific Railroad town.
county_metadata.research_tips: Dawson County Clerk and Recorder in Glendive holds records from 1869 reorganization, though effective records begin later. Glendive was a Northern Pacific Railroad division point — railroad employment and land sale records at the Minnesota Historical Society and MHS identify early settlers. Fort Buford (just across the North Dakota border) records at NARA document military families in the region. The Frontier Gateway Museum in Glendive has local genealogy files. Dawson County was reorganized multiple times as parent to several eastern Montana counties. Irrigation along the Yellowstone River attracted farmers; Bureau of Reclamation records at NARA Denver document water rights. The Glendive Ranger Review newspaper archives are on microfilm at MHS.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1869 to present. County Clerk and Recorder maintains index books and recorded instruments.
Marriage licenses and certificates from 1869. County Clerk and Recorder issued licenses; state registration from 1943.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: State registration from 1907; county-filed records may predate 1907. Certified copies from Montana DPHHS Vital Records. Some counties have earlier records filed with the county clerk.
Civil and criminal case files, probate, guardianship, adoption, naturalization, and name changes from 1869.
Microfilmed county records including deeds, marriages, and probate. Browse-only image collections from Dawson County Clerk and Recorder's office.
Federal population schedules for Dawson County. Montana Territory enumerated from 1870. Agricultural and manufacturing schedules provide additional family details.
Historical newspapers from Dawson County communities. Montana Historical Society Newspaper Archives and Chronicling America have digitized Montana papers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1865 as one of the original nine Montana Territory counties. Consolidated with Anaconda as Anaconda-Deer Lodge County in 1977. Treat as single jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Deer Lodge County (Anaconda-Deer Lodge County since 1977 consolidation) Clerk and Recorder in Anaconda holds records from 1865. The Anaconda Copper Mining Company smelter dominated the town — company employment records, payroll ledgers, and union records at MHS and University of Montana archives are essential. The smelter attracted Irish, Croatian, Serbian, Finnish, Italian, and Cornish immigrants; ethnic parish records (St. Paul's Catholic, St. Mark's Episcopal) are on FamilySearch. The Copper Village Museum in Anaconda has local family files. Montana State Prison records (1871+) at MHS cover inmates from across the state. The Deer Lodge Valley was an early ranching center before the smelter era — stock brand records at MHS identify pioneer stockmen.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1913 from Custer County. Named for Benjamin O'Fallon, Indian agent.
county_metadata.research_tips: Fallon County Clerk and Recorder in Baker has records from 1913. Fallon County was part of the eastern Montana homesteading boom (1909-1918); many homestead claims were filed but later abandoned during drought. The Fallon County Museum (O'Fallon Historical Museum) in Baker has homestead-era photographs and family files. Check Custer County for pre-1913 records. BLM's GLO database has all federal land patents for the county. The Baker Field (oil) discovery in the 1950s brought oil workers; Montana Oil and Gas Conservation Division records document well operators. Ranching families are documented in livestock brand books at the Montana Department of Livestock. The Fallon County Times newspaper is on microfilm at MHS.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1885 from parts of Meagher and Chouteau counties. Central Montana cattle and farming center.
county_metadata.research_tips: Fergus County Clerk and Recorder in Lewistown holds records from 1885. Lewistown was settled by Metis families from Manitoba in the 1870s and 1880s — St. Leo's Catholic Church records document this community. The Central Montana Historical Association Museum in Lewistown has pioneer family collections. Fergus County was a major open-range cattle area; the Judith Basin Roundup records at MHS identify cowboys and ranchers. Gold mining at Maiden and Gilt Edge produced mining district records at MHS. The 1886-87 winter killed most open-range cattle herds — stock loss claims document affected ranchers. Croatian and other immigrant families worked in the Judith Mountains mines. Check Meagher and Chouteau counties for pre-1885 records. The Lewistown Democrat-News archives are on microfilm at MHS.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1893 from Missoula County. Gateway to Glacier National Park. Named for the Flathead (Salish) Indians.
county_metadata.research_tips: Flathead County Clerk and Recorder in Kalispell has records from 1893. Kalispell was founded as a Great Northern Railway division point in 1891 — James J. Hill's railroad brought settlers from Scandinavia and the Upper Midwest. The Northwest Montana History Museum in Kalispell has local genealogy collections. Glacier National Park records (1910+) at NARA document rangers, concessionaires, and construction workers. The Columbia Falls Aluminum Company and timber industry employed many families. Flathead Lake communities (Polson, Bigfork, Somers) have distinct settlement histories. Check Missoula County for pre-1893 records. The Flathead County Library genealogy collection includes surname files and cemetery indexes. The Daily Inter Lake newspaper archives (1889+) are on microfilm.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1865 as one of the original nine Montana Territory counties. Home to Montana State University (1893).
county_metadata.research_tips: Gallatin County Clerk and Recorder in Bozeman holds deed records from 1865. Bozeman was founded in 1864 along the Bozeman Trail. The Gallatin Historical Society and Pioneer Museum in Bozeman has extensive family collections. Montana State University (1893) alumni and faculty records provide genealogical data. Fort Ellis (1867-1886) military records at NARA document soldiers assigned to protect the Bozeman Trail. The Gallatin Valley was one of Montana's earliest agricultural areas — territorial agricultural census records at MHS. Three Forks area was the headwaters fur trade center; Lewis and Clark expedition records reference the area. The Bozeman Trail conflicts with the Lakota and Cheyenne (1866-68) are documented in military records at NARA. The Bozeman Chronicle newspaper archives extend to 1883.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1919 from Dawson County. One of Montana's most remote and least-populated counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Garfield County Clerk and Recorder in Jordan has records from 1919. Garfield County is one of the most isolated and sparsely populated counties in the continental US. The Garfield County Museum in Jordan holds local ranching and homesteading records. The homestead boom (1909-1918) brought settlers, but severe drought caused massive abandonment in the 1920s; BLM's GLO records document original homestead entries. Check Dawson County for pre-1919 records. The Hell Creek Formation fossil area attracted scientific expeditions; expedition records at museums may name local guides and landowners. Livestock brand records at the Montana Department of Livestock identify ranching families. The county's extreme remoteness makes local newspaper archives (the Jordan Tribune) essential for family research.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1919 from Teton County. Contains the Blackfeet Indian Reservation.
county_metadata.research_tips: Glacier County Clerk and Recorder in Cut Bank holds records from 1919. The Blackfeet Indian Reservation covers most of the county — BIA Blackfeet Agency records at NARA Denver include census rolls (1885-1940), allotment records, school attendance (Blackfeet Boarding School, Cut Bank Boarding School), and agency correspondence. The Museum of the Plains Indian in Browning has cultural and historical collections. Oil and gas exploration on the reservation and the Cut Bank oil field brought energy workers; Montana Oil and Gas Conservation Division records document operators. Check Teton County for pre-1919 records. Glacier National Park (1910+) records at NARA document Blackfeet interactions and park employees. The Great Northern Railway Hi-Line brought homesteaders to the eastern portion of the county.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1920 from Musselshell and Sweet Grass counties. One of Montana's smallest counties by population.
county_metadata.research_tips: Golden Valley County Clerk and Recorder in Ryegate maintains records from 1920. Golden Valley County was created during the homesteading era and quickly lost population during the 1920s drought. The Milwaukee Road railroad (Chicago, Milwaukee, St. Paul and Pacific) ran through the county, bringing settlers — railroad land records at MHS identify purchasers. Check Musselshell and Sweet Grass counties for pre-1920 records. The extremely small population (consistently under 1,000) means that local newspaper archives and county commissioner meeting minutes are especially valuable for identifying all families. BLM's GLO records document original homestead patents. Ryegate was founded by a colonization company that recruited Scandinavian farmers; company promotional materials at MHS name early settlers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1893 from Deer Lodge County. Named for the Granite Mine, one of the richest silver mines in the world.
county_metadata.research_tips: Granite County Clerk and Recorder in Philipsburg holds records from 1893. The Granite Mine and the Bi-Metallic Mine near Philipsburg were among the world's richest silver mines in the 1880s-90s; mine company records and payroll ledgers at MHS and University of Montana archives identify miners. The 1893 silver crash devastated the county. The Granite County Museum in Philipsburg has mining-era family files. Cornish, Irish, and Scandinavian miners are documented in ethnic church records. Check Deer Lodge County for pre-1893 records. Georgetown Lake area homestead and mining claim records at BLM. Flint Creek Valley ranching records predate mining; livestock brand books at MHS identify early stockmen. The Philipsburg Mail newspaper archives are on microfilm at MHS.
